questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Transduction is the process by which:

choices.text: the brain interprets sensory information
choices.explanation: Interpreting sensory information is perception, not transduction. Transduction specifically refers to converting physical energy into neural signals.

choices.text: physical energy is converted into neural impulses
choices.explanation: Transduction is the conversion of one form of energy (light, sound, pressure, chemicals) into neural impulses that the nervous system can process.

choices.text: sensory adaptation reduces sensitivity to a constant stimulus
choices.explanation: Sensory adaptation is a separate process where sensitivity decreases with constant stimulation. Transduction is the initial conversion of energy into neural signals.

choices.text: attention is directed to a specific stimulus
choices.explanation: Directing attention to a stimulus is selective attention, not transduction.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Which photoreceptors are responsible for color vision and fine detail?

choices.text: Rods
choices.explanation: Rods detect dim light and enable peripheral and night vision, but they do not detect color or provide fine visual detail.

choices.text: Cones
choices.explanation: Cones, concentrated at the fovea, detect color and fine detail. They function best in bright light and come in three types (red, green, blue) per the trichromatic theory.

choices.text: Bipolar cells
choices.explanation: Bipolar cells are intermediate neurons in the retina that connect photoreceptors to ganglion cells — they are not the photoreceptors themselves.

choices.text: Ganglion cells
choices.explanation: Ganglion cells collect signals from bipolar cells and form the optic nerve — they do not directly detect light or color.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: The absolute threshold is defined as the:

choices.text: maximum intensity a stimulus can reach before causing pain
choices.explanation: The maximum tolerable intensity is not the absolute threshold. The absolute threshold refers to the minimum detectable intensity.

choices.text: minimum stimulus intensity detected 50% of the time
choices.explanation: The absolute threshold is the minimum stimulus intensity needed for a person to detect the stimulus 50% of the time under ideal conditions.

choices.text: point at which a stimulus becomes consciously perceived 100% of the time
choices.explanation: The absolute threshold uses the 50% detection criterion, not 100%. Detection varies due to neural noise and other factors.

choices.text: minimum change in stimulus needed to detect a difference
choices.explanation: The minimum detectable change is the difference threshold (just noticeable difference), not the absolute threshold.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: The Gestalt principle of proximity states that we tend to:

choices.text: perceive smooth, continuous patterns rather than abrupt changes
choices.explanation: Perceiving smooth, continuous patterns describes the Gestalt principle of continuity, not proximity.

choices.text: fill in gaps to perceive complete figures
choices.explanation: Filling in gaps to perceive complete figures describes the Gestalt principle of closure, not proximity.

choices.text: group objects that are near each other as belonging together
choices.explanation: The proximity principle states that objects physically near each other are perceived as grouped together, even if they differ in shape or color.

choices.text: group objects that look alike
choices.explanation: Grouping objects that look alike describes the Gestalt principle of similarity, not proximity.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Bottom-up processing involves:

choices.text: using prior knowledge to interpret sensory input
choices.explanation: Using prior knowledge and expectations to interpret sensory input is top-down processing, not bottom-up.

choices.text: building perception from raw sensory data without preconceptions
choices.explanation: Bottom-up (data-driven) processing starts with sensory receptors detecting stimuli and works up to the brain's interpretation, relying on the incoming data rather than expectations.

choices.text: consciously directing attention to a specific stimulus
choices.explanation: Consciously directing attention is selective attention, which can involve both top-down and bottom-up processing.

choices.text: adapting to a constant stimulus over time
choices.explanation: Adapting to a constant stimulus is sensory adaptation, not bottom-up processing.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: The cochlea is part of which sensory system?

choices.text: Vision
choices.explanation: The visual system uses the retina for transduction. The cochlea is located in the inner ear.

choices.text: Hearing
choices.explanation: The cochlea is the fluid-filled structure in the inner ear containing hair cells that transduce sound vibrations into neural signals for hearing.

choices.text: Taste
choices.explanation: Taste uses taste buds on the tongue. The cochlea is an auditory structure.

choices.text: Touch
choices.explanation: Touch is detected by mechanoreceptors in the skin. The cochlea processes sound, not touch.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Sensory adaptation occurs when:

choices.text: a person becomes more sensitive to a stimulus over time
choices.explanation: Sensory adaptation involves decreasing sensitivity, not increasing it. Becoming more sensitive to stimuli is called sensitization.

choices.text: constant stimulation leads to diminished sensitivity to that stimulus
choices.explanation: Sensory adaptation is the reduced sensitivity that occurs when a stimulus remains constant — for example, no longer noticing the ticking of a clock you have been hearing continuously.

choices.text: a person cannot detect any stimuli below the absolute threshold
choices.explanation: The inability to detect stimuli below threshold is simply the definition of threshold, not sensory adaptation.

choices.text: the brain misinterprets sensory information
choices.explanation: Misinterpreting sensory information describes perceptual illusions, not sensory adaptation.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: The monocular depth cue in which parallel lines appear to converge in the distance is called:

choices.text: interposition
choices.explanation: Interposition is the depth cue where one object partially blocks another, indicating which is closer. Converging lines are a different cue.

choices.text: linear perspective
choices.explanation: Linear perspective is the monocular depth cue where parallel lines (like railroad tracks or hallways) appear to converge as they recede into the distance.

choices.text: retinal disparity
choices.explanation: Retinal disparity is a binocular cue based on the different images each eye receives — it requires two eyes and is not about converging lines.

choices.text: texture gradient
choices.explanation: Texture gradient is the depth cue where distant objects appear to have finer, less distinct texture. It does not describe converging lines.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Which sense does NOT pass through the thalamus before reaching the cortex?

choices.text: Vision
choices.explanation: Visual information is relayed through the thalamus (specifically the lateral geniculate nucleus) to the visual cortex.

choices.text: Hearing
choices.explanation: Auditory information passes through the thalamus (specifically the medial geniculate nucleus) before reaching the auditory cortex.

choices.text: Touch
choices.explanation: Touch information is relayed through the thalamus to the somatosensory cortex.

choices.text: Smell
choices.explanation: Olfaction (smell) is the only sense that bypasses the thalamus, sending signals directly from olfactory receptors to the olfactory cortex and limbic system.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Perceptual constancy refers to the tendency to perceive objects as:

choices.text: maintaining their properties (size, shape, color) despite changes in the retinal image
choices.explanation: Perceptual constancy is the brain's tendency to perceive objects as having stable properties even when the sensory input changes — for example, a door appears rectangular even when viewed at an angle.

choices.text: changing in size as they move closer or farther away
choices.explanation: This describes the changing retinal image, which is what perceptual constancy overrides. The brain perceives the object as the same size despite retinal image changes.

choices.text: always being in the same location
choices.explanation: Perceptual constancy involves stable perception of object properties (size, shape, color), not stable perception of location.

choices.text: appearing as part of a larger group based on proximity
choices.explanation: Grouping by proximity is a Gestalt principle of perceptual organization, not perceptual constancy.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: A psychologist conducts an experiment where participants listen to very faint tones in a noisy room. Some participants are told they will receive $20 for each correct detection. Others are told nothing about compensation.

questions.question: This experiment best illustrates which concept?

choices.text: Weber's law
choices.explanation: Weber's law describes the constant proportion needed for a just noticeable difference. This scenario is about motivation affecting detection, not proportional change detection.

choices.text: Signal detection theory
choices.explanation: Signal detection theory predicts that detection depends not only on signal strength but also on motivation, expectations, and response bias. The monetary incentive changes participants' decision criteria for reporting a detection.

choices.text: Sensory adaptation
choices.explanation: Sensory adaptation involves decreased sensitivity to constant stimulation, not motivational effects on detection.

choices.text: Perceptual set
choices.explanation: Perceptual set involves expectations influencing interpretation of stimuli, not motivation affecting detection thresholds in noisy conditions.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: According to Weber's law, if you can just barely notice the difference between a 10-pound weight and an 11-pound weight, you would need a weight of approximately how many pounds to notice a difference from a 50-pound weight?

choices.text: 51 pounds
choices.explanation: Weber's law states the JND is a constant proportion. The proportion here is $1/10 = 0.10$. For 50 lbs: $50 \times 0.10 = 5$ lbs difference needed, so 55 lbs, not 51.

choices.text: 55 pounds
choices.explanation: Weber's law: $\Delta I / I = k$. The constant proportion is $1/10 = 0.10$. For a 50-lb weight: $50 \times 0.10 = 5$ lb. So you would need a 55-lb weight to notice a difference.

choices.text: 60 pounds
choices.explanation: This would imply a Weber fraction of $10/50 = 0.20$, but the original data gives $k = 0.10$. The correct answer is 55 lbs.

choices.text: 100 pounds
choices.explanation: This would imply a Weber fraction of $50/50 = 1.0$, far greater than the established $k = 0.10$. The answer is 55 lbs.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: While studying in a coffee shop, you initially notice the strong smell of coffee. After 20 minutes, you no longer notice the smell, even though it is still present.

questions.question: This experience is best explained by:

choices.text: signal detection theory
choices.explanation: Signal detection theory explains how detection varies with motivation and expectations, not the gradual fading of awareness of a constant stimulus.

choices.text: sensory adaptation
choices.explanation: Sensory adaptation is the reduced sensitivity to an unchanging stimulus. Olfactory receptors adapt to the constant coffee smell, so you stop consciously noticing it.

choices.text: perceptual set
choices.explanation: Perceptual set involves expectations influencing what you perceive, not the fading of awareness of a constant stimulus.

choices.text: the opponent-process theory
choices.explanation: Opponent-process theory explains color vision through opposing color pairs, not the adaptation of smell receptors.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: The trichromatic theory and the opponent-process theory of color vision are:

choices.text: contradictory, and only one can be correct
choices.explanation: The two theories are not contradictory — they describe different stages of color processing and are both supported by evidence.

choices.text: complementary, explaining color processing at different stages of the visual system
choices.explanation: Trichromatic theory explains color detection at the retinal level (three cone types), while opponent-process theory explains color processing at the neural level (ganglion cells and the brain). Both are correct at their respective stages.

choices.text: identical theories using different terminology
choices.explanation: The theories propose fundamentally different mechanisms — three cone types vs. three opponent pairs — at different levels of processing.

choices.text: both disproven by modern neuroscience research
choices.explanation: Both theories have strong empirical support. Trichromatic theory is confirmed by three cone types; opponent-process theory is confirmed by neural opponent channels.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: A musician gradually loses the ability to hear high-pitched sounds after years of performing at loud concerts. An audiologist determines that the damage is in the inner ear.

questions.question: This type of hearing loss is classified as:

choices.text: conduction hearing loss
choices.explanation: Conduction hearing loss involves problems with the outer or middle ear structures that conduct sound (eardrum, ossicles). Inner ear damage is sensorineural.

choices.text: sensorineural hearing loss
choices.explanation: Sensorineural hearing loss results from damage to the cochlea's hair cells or the auditory nerve. Prolonged loud noise destroys hair cells, which do not regenerate, causing permanent hearing loss.

choices.text: auditory masking
choices.explanation: Auditory masking occurs when one sound makes another harder to hear simultaneously — it is not a type of permanent hearing loss.

choices.text: sensory adaptation
choices.explanation: Sensory adaptation is temporary reduced sensitivity to constant stimulation. This musician has permanent structural damage, not adaptation.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Place theory best explains our perception of:

choices.text: low-pitched sounds
choices.explanation: Low-pitched sounds are better explained by frequency theory, where the basilar membrane vibrates at the same rate as the sound wave.

choices.text: high-pitched sounds
choices.explanation: Place theory explains high-pitched sound perception: different frequencies cause peak vibration at different locations along the basilar membrane, and the brain reads pitch from which hair cells are most active.

choices.text: the direction of sounds
choices.explanation: Sound localization depends on interaural time and intensity differences between the two ears, not place theory.

choices.text: the loudness of sounds
choices.explanation: Loudness is primarily coded by the number of hair cells activated and the rate of neural firing, not the specific location of basilar membrane vibration.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: Lisa is reading a text message from her friend that contains several typos and autocorrect errors. Despite the mistakes, she easily understands the message because she can fill in the correct words based on context.

questions.question: Lisa's ability to understand the message best demonstrates:

choices.text: bottom-up processing
choices.explanation: Bottom-up processing relies on raw sensory data. Lisa is using context and expectations to interpret garbled text, which is the opposite approach.

choices.text: top-down processing
choices.explanation: Top-down processing uses prior knowledge, expectations, and context to interpret sensory input. Lisa fills in the correct meaning despite typos because her brain applies contextual knowledge.

choices.text: sensory adaptation
choices.explanation: Sensory adaptation involves decreased sensitivity to constant stimulation and is unrelated to reading comprehension of garbled text.

choices.text: the absolute threshold
choices.explanation: The absolute threshold concerns detecting minimum stimulus intensity. Lisa's challenge is interpretation, not detection.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Retinal disparity is a binocular depth cue that relies on:

choices.text: the slightly different images each eye receives due to their different positions
choices.explanation: Because the eyes are about 2.5 inches apart, each receives a slightly different image. The brain uses this retinal disparity to calculate depth — greater disparity indicates the object is closer.

choices.text: the degree to which both eyes turn inward when focusing on a nearby object
choices.explanation: The inward turning of the eyes is convergence, a different binocular cue from retinal disparity.

choices.text: one object blocking part of another to indicate relative depth
choices.explanation: One object blocking another is interposition, a monocular depth cue that does not require both eyes.

choices.text: objects farther away appearing smaller on the retina
choices.explanation: Farther objects appearing smaller is relative size, a monocular depth cue, not binocular retinal disparity.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: A researcher shows participants an ambiguous image that can be seen as either a young woman or an old woman. Before viewing the image, half the participants are shown photos of elderly people, and half are shown photos of young people.

questions.question: If participants who viewed elderly photos are more likely to see the old woman in the ambiguous image, this would best demonstrate:

choices.text: sensory adaptation
choices.explanation: Sensory adaptation involves reduced sensitivity from constant stimulation, not priming effects on perceptual interpretation.

choices.text: perceptual set
choices.explanation: Perceptual set is a mental predisposition to perceive stimuli in a particular way based on expectations, experience, or context. Being primed with elderly photos creates an expectation that biases perception.

choices.text: the absolute threshold
choices.explanation: The absolute threshold involves detecting minimum stimulus intensity. This scenario concerns interpretation of an ambiguous stimulus, not detection.

choices.text: Weber's law
choices.explanation: Weber's law describes the proportional relationship for just noticeable differences, not the influence of priming on perception.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: The gate-control theory of pain suggests that:

choices.text: pain is entirely determined by the intensity of the physical stimulus
choices.explanation: Gate-control theory explicitly argues against this — psychological factors and non-pain signals can modulate the perception of pain.

choices.text: a neural gate in the spinal cord can block pain signals from reaching the brain, influenced by non-pain signals and cognitive factors
choices.explanation: Gate-control theory (Melzack & Wall) proposes that small nerve fiber signals open the pain gate, while large fiber signals (like rubbing a bumped shin) and brain signals (like distraction or positive emotions) can close it.

choices.text: pain perception adapts completely after prolonged exposure to a painful stimulus
choices.explanation: Unlike other senses, pain does not fully adapt — persistent pain signals continue to reach the brain. Gate-control theory describes modulation, not complete adaptation.

choices.text: pain is processed exclusively in the thalamus
choices.explanation: While the thalamus relays pain signals, gate-control theory focuses on modulation at the spinal cord level, and pain perception involves multiple brain regions.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: Daniel Simons and Christopher Chabris conducted an experiment in which participants watched a video of people passing basketballs and were asked to count the number of passes made by the team wearing white shirts. During the video, a person in a gorilla suit walked through the scene.

questions.question: About half the participants failed to notice the gorilla. This finding demonstrates:

choices.text: change blindness
choices.explanation: Change blindness is failing to detect changes in visual scenes during brief disruptions. The gorilla was a continuously visible unexpected event, not a change between views.

choices.text: inattentional blindness
choices.explanation: Inattentional blindness is the failure to notice a fully visible but unexpected object when attention is focused on another task. Participants were so focused on counting passes that they did not notice the gorilla.

choices.text: sensory adaptation
choices.explanation: Sensory adaptation involves decreased sensitivity to constant stimulation. The gorilla was a novel stimulus, not a constant one.

choices.text: the blind spot
choices.explanation: The blind spot is the point on the retina where the optic nerve exits. Missing the gorilla was due to attentional, not retinal, factors.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: The vestibular sense provides information about:

choices.text: body position and limb movement
choices.explanation: Body position and limb movement information comes from kinesthesia (proprioception), which uses receptors in muscles, tendons, and joints.

choices.text: balance, body orientation, and head movement
choices.explanation: The vestibular sense, based in the semicircular canals and vestibular sacs of the inner ear, detects head position, rotation, and movement to maintain balance and spatial orientation.

choices.text: skin pressure and temperature
choices.explanation: Skin pressure and temperature are detected by somatosensory receptors (touch), not the vestibular system.

choices.text: pain intensity and location
choices.explanation: Pain detection involves nociceptors and pain pathways, not the vestibular system.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: When you stare at a red square for 30 seconds and then look at a white wall, you perceive a green afterimage.

questions.question: This afterimage effect is best explained by:

choices.text: trichromatic theory
choices.explanation: Trichromatic theory explains that we have three cone types (red, green, blue), but it does not account for why we see opponent-color afterimages.

choices.text: opponent-process theory
choices.explanation: Opponent-process theory explains afterimages: staring at red fatigues the red-green opponent channel's red component, so when you look away, the green component dominates, producing a green afterimage.

choices.text: feature detection theory
choices.explanation: Feature detection involves neurons responding to specific visual features (lines, edges, angles), not color afterimage phenomena.

choices.text: place theory
choices.explanation: Place theory describes how pitch is perceived through basilar membrane vibration location — it is a hearing theory, not a vision theory.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Kinesthesia differs from the vestibular sense in that kinesthesia:

choices.text: uses receptors in the inner ear to detect balance
choices.explanation: Inner ear receptors for balance are part of the vestibular sense, not kinesthesia.

choices.text: uses receptors in muscles, tendons, and joints to detect body position and movement
choices.explanation: Kinesthesia (proprioception) relies on receptors in muscles, tendons, and joints to provide information about the position and movement of body parts. The vestibular sense uses inner ear structures for balance.

choices.text: detects only touch and pressure on the skin
choices.explanation: Touch and pressure detection is handled by somatosensory receptors in the skin (cutaneous sense), not kinesthetic receptors.

choices.text: processes only visual depth information
choices.explanation: Visual depth processing uses retinal and cortical mechanisms for vision, not kinesthetic receptors.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: The figure-ground Gestalt principle refers to the tendency to:

choices.text: group similar objects together
choices.explanation: Grouping similar objects is the Gestalt principle of similarity, not figure-ground.

choices.text: perceive smooth, continuous lines
choices.explanation: Perceiving smooth, continuous lines is the principle of continuity, not figure-ground.

choices.text: organize perception into a main object (figure) against a background (ground)
choices.explanation: Figure-ground organization is the fundamental Gestalt principle of separating a visual scene into a prominent figure and a less prominent background, as in the classic vase-faces illusion.

choices.text: fill in missing parts of an incomplete figure
choices.explanation: Filling in missing parts describes the principle of closure, not figure-ground.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: A child who has had cataracts from birth has surgery to restore vision at age 10. After surgery, the child can see colors and detect motion but has difficulty recognizing faces and interpreting spatial relationships.

questions.question: This case study best supports the idea that:

choices.text: vision is entirely innate and does not require experience
choices.explanation: If vision were entirely innate, the child would immediately perceive normally after surgery. The perceptual difficulties demonstrate that experience plays a role.

choices.text: there is a critical period for the development of certain visual perceptual abilities
choices.explanation: The child can detect basic sensory features but cannot interpret complex patterns, suggesting a critical period during which visual experience is needed for normal perceptual development of face recognition and spatial relationships.

choices.text: bottom-up processing alone is sufficient for normal perception
choices.explanation: The child has intact bottom-up processing (detecting colors, motion) but lacks developed top-down processing for complex interpretation, showing both are needed.

choices.text: sensory adaptation prevents normal vision in all restored-sight patients
choices.explanation: The issue is not sensory adaptation but lack of perceptual learning during a critical developmental period.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Feature detectors in the visual cortex respond to:

choices.text: specific visual features such as edges, lines, and angles
choices.explanation: Feature detectors, discovered by Hubel and Wiesel, are neurons in the visual cortex that respond selectively to specific visual features like edges, bars, and angles at particular orientations.

choices.text: the overall brightness of the visual field
choices.explanation: Overall brightness is processed by more basic retinal mechanisms, not the specialized feature detection neurons in the visual cortex.

choices.text: only moving objects in the visual field
choices.explanation: Some feature detectors respond to motion, but they also respond to stationary features like specific line orientations and edges.

choices.text: only color information from cone receptors
choices.explanation: Color processing involves cone receptors and color-opponent cells. Feature detectors respond to spatial features like edges and orientations, not exclusively to color.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: The blind spot in each eye exists because:

choices.text: the fovea has a gap where no photoreceptors are located
choices.explanation: The fovea is actually the area of highest cone density and sharpest vision. The blind spot is elsewhere on the retina.

choices.text: the optic nerve exits the retina at a point where there are no photoreceptors
choices.explanation: The blind spot occurs where the optic nerve leaves the eye, creating a gap in the retina with no rods or cones. The brain fills in this gap using information from the surrounding area and the other eye.

choices.text: the lens cannot focus light on the entire retina
choices.explanation: The lens can focus light across the entire retina. The blind spot is caused by the physical structure of the optic nerve exit, not a focusing limitation.

choices.text: the iris blocks some light from reaching the retina
choices.explanation: The iris controls pupil size to regulate light amount but does not create the blind spot. The blind spot is at the optic disc.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Size constancy would be most challenged when:

choices.text: viewing a familiar object at a known distance
choices.explanation: Familiar objects at known distances easily maintain size constancy because the brain can use experience to judge size accurately.

choices.text: looking at unfamiliar objects at great distances with no other depth cues
choices.explanation: Size constancy depends on depth cues and familiarity. Without these cues (like when viewing unfamiliar objects at great distances in a featureless environment), the brain cannot accurately maintain size constancy.

choices.text: viewing objects under bright lighting conditions
choices.explanation: Bright lighting enhances visual perception and provides more cues for size constancy, not fewer.

choices.text: looking at stationary objects from a fixed position
choices.explanation: A stable viewing position with consistent depth cues supports size constancy rather than challenging it.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Accommodation in vision refers to:

choices.text: the process by which the lens changes shape to focus on objects at different distances
choices.explanation: Accommodation is the process by which ciliary muscles change the shape of the lens — flattening it for distant objects and rounding it for near objects — to focus images sharply on the retina.

choices.text: the adjustment of the pupil to changes in light intensity
choices.explanation: Pupil adjustment to light is the pupillary reflex (dilation and constriction), controlled by the iris. Accommodation specifically refers to lens shape changes for focusing.

choices.text: the brain's adaptation to visual stimuli over time
choices.explanation: Adaptation to visual stimuli over time is sensory adaptation, not accommodation. Accommodation is a physical lens adjustment.

choices.text: the inward turning of the eyes when focusing on close objects
choices.explanation: Inward turning of the eyes is convergence, a binocular depth cue. Accommodation is the lens shape change for focusing.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Motion parallax is a depth cue based on:

choices.text: the fact that closer objects appear to move faster across the visual field when the observer is in motion
choices.explanation: Motion parallax occurs because nearby objects appear to sweep past more quickly than distant objects when you are moving (e.g., looking out a car window). This relative motion difference provides a monocular depth cue.

choices.text: the slightly different images received by each eye
choices.explanation: Different images from each eye is retinal disparity, a binocular cue. Motion parallax is monocular and based on observer movement.

choices.text: the convergence of parallel lines in the distance
choices.explanation: Converging parallel lines is linear perspective, a different monocular cue. Motion parallax specifically involves apparent speed differences during movement.

choices.text: one object partially blocking another
choices.explanation: Object blocking is interposition, a monocular cue that does not involve observer movement.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: If a person is red-green colorblind, the most likely explanation according to color vision theory is:

choices.text: they are missing or have defective red or green cones, as explained by trichromatic theory
choices.explanation: Trichromatic theory explains color blindness as a deficiency in one or more cone types. Red-green colorblindness results from absent or defective red or green cones, preventing normal discrimination of these colors.

choices.text: their rods are unable to detect colored light
choices.explanation: Rods do not detect color at all — they function in dim light for black-and-white vision. Color blindness is a cone deficiency.

choices.text: the optic nerve cannot transmit color information to the brain
choices.explanation: The optic nerve transmits all visual information. Color blindness originates at the receptor (cone) level, not the neural transmission level.

choices.text: their visual cortex cannot process any visual information
choices.explanation: Color-blind individuals process visual information normally; only specific color discrimination is affected due to cone deficiency.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: Dr. Martinez conducts an experiment where radiologists examine chest X-rays for lung nodules. Unknown to them, a small image of a gorilla (48 times the size of an average nodule) is inserted into one of the X-rays. 83% of the radiologists fail to notice the gorilla, despite their eyes fixating directly on its location.

questions.question: This finding most directly challenges which assumption?

choices.text: Expert attention guarantees detection of unexpected stimuli
choices.explanation: This study (Drew, Vo, & Wolfe, 2013) demonstrates that even experts are susceptible to inattentional blindness. Focused attention on a specific task (finding nodules) can cause failure to detect unexpected stimuli, even large ones directly fixated upon.

choices.text: Sensory adaptation only occurs with non-visual stimuli
choices.explanation: The radiologists' failure was due to inattentional blindness (attention-based), not sensory adaptation (receptor-based). This finding does not address sensory adaptation.

choices.text: Bottom-up processing is always more reliable than top-down processing
choices.explanation: While the study involves top-down attentional effects, its primary contribution is showing that expertise does not protect against inattentional blindness, not comparing processing types.

choices.text: The absolute threshold varies based on stimulus size
choices.explanation: The gorilla was clearly above the absolute threshold (very large and visible). The failure was attentional, not a threshold issue.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: According to signal detection theory, a 'false alarm' occurs when a person:

choices.text: correctly detects a stimulus that is present
choices.explanation: Correctly detecting a present stimulus is a 'hit' in signal detection theory, not a false alarm.

choices.text: reports detecting a stimulus when no stimulus is actually present
choices.explanation: A false alarm (false positive) occurs when a person says 'yes, I detected the stimulus' when none was present. This is influenced by the person's response bias and decision criteria.

choices.text: fails to detect a stimulus that is present
choices.explanation: Failing to detect a present stimulus is a 'miss' (false negative) in signal detection theory, not a false alarm.

choices.text: correctly identifies the absence of a stimulus
choices.explanation: Correctly identifying that no stimulus is present is a 'correct rejection' in signal detection theory, not a false alarm.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: A researcher studies visual cliff experiments with infants from different cultures. Most infants, regardless of culture, show reluctance to crawl over the 'deep side' of the visual cliff once they have crawling experience, even though a glass surface prevents any actual fall.

questions.question: This finding provides evidence that depth perception:

choices.text: is entirely learned through cultural experiences
choices.explanation: If depth perception were entirely culturally learned, there would be significant cultural differences. The cross-cultural consistency suggests a biological or maturational component.

choices.text: involves both innate biological readiness and experience with self-produced locomotion
choices.explanation: The visual cliff studies (Gibson & Walk) show that depth perception has an innate component (fear of heights emerges across cultures) but also requires experience (mobile infants show fear; pre-crawling infants do not), suggesting a nature-nurture interaction.

choices.text: is fully developed at birth and requires no experience
choices.explanation: Pre-crawling infants do not show avoidance of the deep side, suggesting that while the capacity may be innate, experience with locomotion is needed to activate depth-avoidance behavior.

choices.text: depends exclusively on binocular depth cues
choices.explanation: The visual cliff uses multiple depth cues including texture gradient and pattern density (monocular cues). Depth perception does not depend exclusively on binocular cues.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: A key difference between sensory adaptation and habituation is that:

choices.text: sensory adaptation occurs at the receptor level, while habituation occurs at the brain level
choices.explanation: Sensory adaptation is decreased receptor sensitivity to constant stimulation (peripheral process). Habituation is decreased brain response to a repeated stimulus (central process). Both reduce awareness, but through different mechanisms at different levels.

choices.text: habituation only applies to visual stimuli
choices.explanation: Habituation can occur with any repeated stimulus across all senses, not just visual stimuli.

choices.text: sensory adaptation is a conscious, voluntary process
choices.explanation: Sensory adaptation is an automatic, involuntary process that occurs at the receptor level without conscious control.

choices.text: habituation is a permanent change, while sensory adaptation is temporary
choices.explanation: Both habituation and sensory adaptation are reversible — a novel stimulus restores responsiveness in habituation, and removing the constant stimulus restores sensitivity in adaptation.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: A patient reports that she can describe objects placed in her hand by touch but cannot recognize them by sight alone, even though her visual acuity tests are normal and she can see colors and shapes.

questions.question: This condition is most likely:

choices.text: visual agnosia
choices.explanation: Visual agnosia is the inability to recognize objects by sight despite normal visual acuity. The patient can see (intact sensation) but cannot interpret what she sees (impaired visual perception), typically due to damage to visual association areas.

choices.text: blindsight
choices.explanation: Blindsight involves responding to visual stimuli without conscious awareness of seeing them, typically after primary visual cortex damage. This patient is conscious of seeing but cannot recognize objects.

choices.text: color blindness
choices.explanation: The patient can see colors normally. Her difficulty is with object recognition, not color discrimination.

choices.text: sensory adaptation
choices.explanation: Sensory adaptation is temporary reduced sensitivity to constant stimulation. This patient has a persistent inability to recognize objects visually, suggesting brain damage.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: The volley principle was proposed to explain how the auditory system encodes:

choices.text: very high-pitched sounds above 5000 Hz
choices.explanation: Very high-pitched sounds are better explained by place theory (which part of the basilar membrane vibrates). The volley principle addresses intermediate frequencies.

choices.text: sounds in the intermediate frequency range (between 100 and 5000 Hz) by having neurons fire in alternating groups
choices.explanation: The volley principle extends frequency theory: while individual neurons cannot fire faster than about 1000 times per second, groups of neurons can alternate (volley) their firing to collectively match higher frequencies up to about 5000 Hz.

choices.text: the loudness of sounds based on the number of hair cells activated
choices.explanation: Loudness coding involves the number and type of hair cells activated, but the volley principle specifically addresses how pitch (frequency) is encoded, not loudness.

choices.text: sound location using differences between the two ears
choices.explanation: Sound localization uses interaural time and intensity differences. The volley principle addresses pitch encoding, not localization.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: Researchers ask participants to view a Necker cube — a wireframe drawing of a cube that can be perceived as facing in two different orientations. Participants report that their perception spontaneously alternates between the two orientations, even though the physical stimulus does not change.

questions.question: This phenomenon best illustrates that:

choices.text: perception is an active construction of the brain, not a passive recording of stimuli
choices.explanation: The Necker cube demonstrates that the same physical stimulus can produce different perceptual experiences. Since the input is constant but perception changes, this proves the brain actively constructs perception rather than passively recording it.

choices.text: sensory adaptation causes the visual system to alternate between two states
choices.explanation: While neural fatigue may contribute to the alternation, the key insight is that perception is constructed — the same stimulus produces two valid interpretations, showing perception goes beyond simple detection.

choices.text: bottom-up processing always produces a single, accurate percept
choices.explanation: The Necker cube contradicts this claim — bottom-up processing from the same stimulus produces two different percepts, demonstrating that perception is not simply a faithful recording of input.

choices.text: binocular disparity is necessary for depth perception
choices.explanation: The Necker cube is a flat 2D drawing viewed with both eyes seeing the same image. The alternating perception occurs without binocular disparity.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Which of the following is true about subliminal perception?

choices.text: Subliminal stimuli can produce measurable brain responses and slight priming effects, but cannot reliably control complex behavior
choices.explanation: Research shows subliminal stimuli can produce priming effects (e.g., briefly flashed words can influence subsequent judgments) and activate neural responses, but they cannot control buying behavior, attitudes, or complex decisions as once feared.

choices.text: Subliminal advertising has been proven to effectively control consumer purchasing decisions
choices.explanation: Controlled studies have consistently failed to show that subliminal advertising can reliably influence purchasing behavior. The original 'Drink Coca-Cola' study was fabricated.

choices.text: Subliminal stimuli cannot be detected by the brain at all
choices.explanation: Brain imaging studies show that subliminal stimuli do activate neural pathways, even though the person is not consciously aware of them.

choices.text: Subliminal perception only works with auditory stimuli, not visual
choices.explanation: Subliminal perception has been demonstrated with both visual and auditory stimuli in laboratory settings.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: A patient with damage to the primary visual cortex reports being completely blind in part of the visual field. However, when objects are placed in the 'blind' area and the patient is forced to guess their location, the patient points to the correct location at rates significantly above chance.

questions.question: This phenomenon is known as:

choices.text: inattentional blindness
choices.explanation: Inattentional blindness involves failing to notice visible stimuli due to focused attention, not unconscious visual processing after cortical damage.

choices.text: blindsight
choices.explanation: Blindsight occurs when patients with primary visual cortex damage can respond to visual stimuli in their 'blind' field without conscious awareness. Information likely reaches subcortical visual pathways (superior colliculus), enabling unconscious spatial processing.

choices.text: visual agnosia
choices.explanation: Visual agnosia involves inability to recognize objects despite intact vision. This patient reports no conscious vision in the affected area but can unconsciously localize objects.

choices.text: change blindness
choices.explanation: Change blindness is failing to detect visual changes during brief disruptions. This patient has cortical damage causing blindness in a field, yet retains unconscious localization ability.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: The Müller-Lyer illusion, where lines of equal length appear different due to inward or outward arrow fins, is thought to result from:

choices.text: the brain's misapplication of size-distance scaling based on depth cues from the arrow fins
choices.explanation: The leading explanation is that the arrow fins resemble corners of rooms (inside corners appear farther, outside corners closer). The brain applies size-distance scaling, perceiving the line with 'far' cues as longer to compensate for apparent distance — a misapplication of a usually useful perceptual mechanism.

choices.text: damage to the retina's cone cells
choices.explanation: The Müller-Lyer illusion occurs in people with perfectly healthy vision. It is a perceptual phenomenon, not a retinal deficiency.

choices.text: failure of the optic nerve to transmit complete information
choices.explanation: The optic nerve functions normally in people who see this illusion. The effect is caused by brain-level perceptual processing, not neural transmission failure.

choices.text: sensory adaptation to the arrow fin stimuli
choices.explanation: The illusion occurs on first viewing and does not require prolonged exposure. It is a perceptual misapplication, not an adaptation effect.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: A researcher presents the same ambiguous auditory stimulus to two groups. Group A is told they will hear a sentence in English. Group B is told they will hear random noise. Group A is significantly more likely to report hearing meaningful words.

questions.question: This finding best illustrates how _______ influences perception:

choices.text: bottom-up processing
choices.explanation: Both groups received the same physical stimulus. The difference in perception was driven by expectations (top-down), not by the sensory data itself (bottom-up).

choices.text: top-down processing
choices.explanation: The instructions created different expectations, leading Group A to hear meaningful words in noise. This demonstrates top-down processing — expectations and prior knowledge shape how ambiguous sensory input is interpreted.

choices.text: sensory adaptation
choices.explanation: Sensory adaptation involves decreased sensitivity to constant stimulation. This experiment measured perception of a single stimulus presentation, not adaptation over time.

choices.text: the difference threshold
choices.explanation: The difference threshold concerns detecting changes between two stimuli. This experiment is about how expectations influence the interpretation of a single ambiguous stimulus.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Phantom limb pain — where amputees feel pain in a limb that no longer exists — is best explained by:

choices.text: sensory adaptation of the remaining nerve endings
choices.explanation: Sensory adaptation would reduce sensation, not create pain in a missing limb. Phantom limb pain persists because of brain-level processing, not peripheral adaptation.

choices.text: reorganization of the somatosensory cortex, which continues to process signals from the brain region that previously represented the missing limb
choices.explanation: After amputation, the somatosensory cortex area that represented the missing limb may be activated by adjacent cortical regions (neuroplasticity), or residual neural activity may be interpreted as coming from the missing limb, producing phantom sensations and pain.

choices.text: damage to the visual cortex preventing the person from seeing the limb
choices.explanation: Phantom limb pain is a somatosensory phenomenon, not a visual one. The visual cortex is not involved in generating phantom limb sensations.

choices.text: psychological denial of the amputation
choices.explanation: Phantom limb pain has a clear neurological basis in cortical reorganization. It is not a psychological defense mechanism or denial.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: In a signal detection experiment, an airport security screener examines X-ray images of luggage looking for weapons. The screener knows that weapons are extremely rare (less than 1 in 10,000 bags). After hours of screening, the screener has a very high rate of 'misses' (failing to detect weapons that are present in test images).

questions.question: According to signal detection theory, the screener's high miss rate is most likely due to:

choices.text: a liberal response criterion that increases false alarms
choices.explanation: A liberal criterion (tendency to say 'yes') would increase false alarms and hits while decreasing misses. The screener's high miss rate suggests the opposite — a conservative criterion.

choices.text: a conservative response criterion influenced by the extremely low base rate of actual threats
choices.explanation: When threats are extremely rare, screeners develop a conservative response bias (tendency to say 'no threat'), raising the threshold for reporting a detection. This increases misses but reduces false alarms. The low base rate shifts decision criteria.

choices.text: complete sensory adaptation to the X-ray images
choices.explanation: Sensory adaptation would affect perception of all images. The screener can still see the images clearly; the issue is the decision criterion for reporting a threat, not inability to see.

choices.text: a decrease in the screener's absolute threshold for detecting weapons
choices.explanation: A decrease in absolute threshold would make the screener more sensitive, reducing misses. The problem here is response bias, not perceptual sensitivity.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: The fact that smell bypasses the thalamus and connects directly to the limbic system helps explain why:

choices.text: people have difficulty identifying smells by name
choices.explanation: Difficulty naming smells is related to the limited connection between olfactory cortex and language areas, not the thalamic bypass.

choices.text: odors are particularly effective at triggering vivid emotional memories
choices.explanation: Because olfactory signals go directly to the limbic system (including the amygdala and areas near the hippocampus) without thalamic relay, smells have a uniquely strong connection to emotion and memory, explaining why a familiar scent can instantly evoke vivid emotional memories.

choices.text: the sense of smell adapts more slowly than other senses
choices.explanation: Olfactory adaptation actually occurs relatively quickly. The thalamic bypass explains the emotional potency of smell, not its adaptation rate.

choices.text: humans can detect more distinct odors than colors
choices.explanation: While humans can distinguish many odors, this ability relates to the diversity of olfactory receptors, not the thalamic bypass.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Research on the 'cocktail party effect' demonstrates that:

choices.text: people can selectively attend to one voice in a noisy room and detect personally relevant information (like their name) in unattended channels
choices.explanation: The cocktail party effect shows selective attention allows focusing on one voice while filtering others. However, personally meaningful stimuli (like hearing your name) in the unattended channel can break through, demonstrating some processing of unattended information.

choices.text: sensory adaptation makes it impossible to hear in noisy environments
choices.explanation: The cocktail party effect shows we can hear in noisy environments by selectively attending to one source. This contradicts the claim that noise makes hearing impossible.

choices.text: auditory perception relies exclusively on bottom-up processing
choices.explanation: The ability to selectively focus on one conversation demonstrates top-down processing (using expectations and attention to filter input), not exclusively bottom-up processing.

choices.text: all auditory information in the environment is consciously processed equally
choices.explanation: The cocktail party effect demonstrates that we do not process all sounds equally — we selectively attend to some and filter others, though important stimuli can break through.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: A researcher places a participant in a completely dark room and flashes a tiny point of light on the wall. The participant consistently reports that the stationary light appears to move. This is repeated with many participants, all of whom report the same perceived movement.

questions.question: This perceptual phenomenon is called the:

choices.text: phi phenomenon
choices.explanation: The phi phenomenon involves perceiving apparent motion from rapidly alternating stationary stimuli (like marquee lights). This scenario involves a single stationary light in the dark.

choices.text: autokinetic effect
choices.explanation: The autokinetic effect occurs when a stationary point of light in a dark room appears to move because the visual system has no stable reference frame. Without visual context, small eye movements create the illusion that the light is drifting.

choices.text: stroboscopic effect
choices.explanation: The stroboscopic effect creates apparent motion from a rapid series of still images (like a movie). This scenario involves continuous viewing of a single light.

choices.text: change blindness
choices.explanation: Change blindness involves failing to detect changes in visual scenes. The autokinetic effect involves perceiving motion in a stationary stimulus.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: The relationship between physical stimulus intensity and perceived magnitude is described by Stevens' power law, which differs from Fechner's law in that:

choices.text: Stevens' law uses a power function rather than a logarithmic function, and the exponent varies by sense modality
choices.explanation: Fechner's law proposes a logarithmic relationship (sensation increases as the log of intensity). Stevens' power law proposes $S = kI^n$, where the exponent $n$ varies by modality — allowing different senses to have different scaling relationships.

choices.text: Fechner's law only applies to vision, while Stevens' applies to all senses
choices.explanation: Both laws are intended to apply across senses. The key difference is the mathematical relationship (logarithmic vs. power function), not the scope of senses covered.

choices.text: Stevens' law predicts that all senses scale identically
choices.explanation: Stevens' law specifically predicts that different senses scale differently through varying power function exponents — electric shock expands while brightness compresses, for example.

choices.text: Fechner's law is based on modern neuroimaging, while Stevens' uses subjective reports
choices.explanation: Both laws are historically based on psychophysical methods. Fechner's law is older (1860) and Stevens' law is more recent (1957), but neither is based on neuroimaging.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: Cross-cultural research on the Müller-Lyer illusion shows that people in Western cultures (who grow up in 'carpentered' environments with many right angles and rectangular structures) are more susceptible to the illusion than people in non-carpentered environments (e.g., some African cultures with round dwellings).

questions.question: This finding best supports which conclusion about perception?

choices.text: Perception is entirely biologically determined and does not vary across cultures
choices.explanation: Cross-cultural differences in susceptibility directly contradict the claim that perception is entirely biological and invariant across cultures.

choices.text: Perceptual processes can be influenced by culturally specific visual experiences and environmental features
choices.explanation: The reduced susceptibility in non-carpentered cultures shows that perceptual processing is shaped by environmental experience. People who grow up with rectangular structures develop depth interpretation habits that make them more susceptible to the illusion.

choices.text: The Müller-Lyer illusion is not a real perceptual phenomenon
choices.explanation: The illusion is real — it is experienced across cultures. The finding shows cultural variation in its magnitude, not its nonexistence.

choices.text: Bottom-up processing is identical across all human populations
choices.explanation: If bottom-up processing were identical across populations, susceptibility to the illusion would not vary by culture. The cross-cultural differences demonstrate that experience shapes perceptual processing.
